The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends that all women seeking abortion should be provided with high-quality care to minimize complications and maximize health outcomes .

Acute bronchitis refers to an inflammation of the bronchi in the lower respiratory tract caused by viral or bacterial infection. It typically presents with symptoms such as cough (often productive), sore throat, fever, runny nose, wheezing, chest congestion, shortness of breath, and malaise. Symptoms usually last for 7–10 days without treatment but may persist longer if there is a bacterial cause. In most cases, antibiotics are not recommended because it is a self-limiting condition that can be managed at home with rest, fluids, over-the-counter medications for pain relief and symptom management, and close monitoring for signs of worsening. If symptoms do not improve after 3 weeks, a doctor should be consulted.

Adoption is the legal process by which an adult formally transfers all parental rights and responsibilities to another person or couple who will raise the child as their own. This can be a private arrangement between consenting parties or it can involve state intervention due to neglect or abuse of the biological parents. The adoptive parents typically undergo background checks and home visits before being approved for adoption. Once the adoption is finalized, the adoptive parents have full legal custody of the child and are responsible for providing care and support until the child reaches adulthood. In some cases, there may be ongoing contact with the birth family, depending on the preferences of both parties involved.

Angiography is an imaging test that uses X-rays to produce images of blood vessels in the heart or brain. It can be used to diagnose coronary artery disease (CAD), which is a condition where the arteries supplying blood to the heart become narrowed or blocked due to the buildup of plaque made up of fat, cholesterol, calcium, and other substances within the walls of the arteries . CAD is one of the leading causes of death worldwide and can result in chest pain, shortness of breath, and heart attacks .

answers_repaired: Abortion is the intentional termination of a pregnancy by removing fetal tissue from the uterus before 28 weeks' gestation . There are several methods for performing an abortion, including surgical (aspiration curettage or dilation and evacuation) and medical (with medications such as mifepristone and misoprostol). The most common method in the United States is first-trimester aspiration abortion using local anesthesia and sedation . This procedure involves dilating the cervix with forceps and then using suction to remove the embryo and placenta. It typically takes less than 15 minutes to complete and can be performed up until about 13 weeks of pregnancy. Medical abortions involve taking pills that cause chemical miscarriage and can only be done during the first seven weeks of pregnancy.

Acute bronchitis refers to an inflammation of the airways (bronchi) in the lungs caused by infection with viruses or bacteria . It typically presents as coughing that lasts for 10 days or less without other symptoms such as fever, chills, shortness of breath, wheezing, chest pain, or fatigue. Symptoms may be accompanied by thick mucus production and a low-grade fever. In most cases, antibiotics are not necessary because it is usually caused by a virus rather than a bacterial infection. Treatment involves getting plenty of rest, staying hydrated, over-the-counter medications to help manage symptoms, and avoiding smoking and irritants. Most people recover within one to two weeks. If symptoms persist beyond this time frame, further evaluation should be sought from a healthcare provider due to possible complications.

An angiogram is a procedure that involves the use of contrast dye to visualize blood vessels in order to identify any blockages or other abnormalities. It can be used for various parts of the body including coronary arteries (heart), cerebral arteries (head) and peripheral arteries (limbs). The most common type of angiography is cardiac catheterization which uses fluoroscopy to guide thin flexible tubes called catheters through the blood vessel system into the heart chambers. During this procedure, doctors may also perform balloon angioplasty or stent placement if there are narrowed or blocked arteries found.

Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by Bacillus anthracis bacteria that can affect humans and animals. The pathogenesis of anthrax involves three distinct stages: (1) Cutaneous stage: This stage occurs when spores enter through cuts or open wounds in the skin. Once inside the body, they begin to germinate into vegetative forms of B. anthracis. These organisms produce two important virulence factors called lethal factor (LF) and edema factor (EF). LF inhibits cell-mediated immunity while EF causes localized swelling at the site of infection. As the rash progresses, it may develop ulcers that ooze black eschar, which is characteristic of this form of anthrax. If left untreated, cutaneous anthrax can spread to other parts of the body.

This condition is known as anxiety. Anxiety can be experienced as feelings of tension or apprehension accompanied by physical symptoms such as increased heart rate, sweating, and muscle tension. It may also manifest as avoidance behaviors or excessive preoccupation with potential negative outcomes.
reference_answers: An abortion is a procedure to end a pregnancy. It uses medicine or surgery to remove the embryo or fetus and placenta from the uterus. The procedure is done by a licensed health care professional. The decision to end a pregnancy is very personal. If you are thinking of having an abortion, most healthcare providers advise counseling.

Bronchitis is an inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It causes a cough that often brings up mucus. It can also cause shortness of breath, wheezing, a low fever, and chest tightness. There are two main types of bronchitis: acute and chronic. Most cases of acute bronchitis get better within several days. But your cough can last for several weeks after the infection is gone. The same viruses that cause colds and the flu often cause acute bronchitis. These viruses spread through the air when people cough, or though physical contact (for example, on unwashed hands). Being exposed to tobacco smoke, air pollution, dusts, vapors, and fumes can also cause acute bronchitis. Less often, bacteria can also cause acute bronchitis. To diagnose acute bronchitis, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and listen to your breathing. You may also have other tests. Treatments include rest, fluids, and aspirin (for adults) or acetaminophen to treat fever. A humidifier or steam can also help. You may need inhaled medicine to open your airways if you are wheezing. Antibiotics won't help if the cause is viral. You may get antibiotics if the cause is bacterial. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Summary : Adoption brings a child born to other parents into a new family. Birth parents have a number of reasons for placing children for adoption. Overall, they want better lives for their children than they think they can give them. Children who are eligible for adoption come from many different settings. Some are in foster care, a temporary home setting. Other children live in orphanages or with birth relatives until they can be adopted. There are different kinds of adoption. Children may be adopted by a relative or a new family. Some parents adopt children from the U.S, and some adopt from abroad.

After any operation, you'll have some side effects. There is usually some pain with surgery. There may also be swelling and soreness around the area that the surgeon cut. Your surgeon can tell you which side effects to expect. There can also be complications. These are unplanned events linked to the operation. Some complications are infection, too much bleeding, reaction to anesthesia, or accidental injury. Some people have a greater risk of complications because of other medical conditions. Your surgeon can tell you how you might feel and what you will be able to do - or not do - the first few days, weeks, or months after surgery. Some other questions to ask are - How long you will be in the hospital - What kind of supplies, equipment, and help you might need when you go home - When you can go back to work - When it is ok to start exercising again - Are they any other restrictions in your activities Following your surgeon's advice can help you recover as soon as possible. Agency for Healthcare Quality and Research

Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency) is an inherited condition that raises your risk for lung and liver disease. Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) is a protein that protects the lungs. The liver makes it. If the AAT proteins aren't the right shape, they get stuck in the liver cells and can't reach the lungs. Symptoms of AAT deficiency include - Shortness of breath and wheezing - Repeated lung infections - Tiredness - Rapid heartbeat upon standing - Vision problems - Weight loss Some people have no symptoms and do not develop complications. Blood tests and genetic tests can tell if you have it. If your lungs are affected, you may also have lung tests. Treatments include medicines, pulmonary rehab, and extra oxygen, if needed. Severe cases may need a lung transplant. Not smoking can prevent or delay lung symptoms. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a nervous system disease that attacks nerve cells called neurons in your brain and spinal cord. These neurons transmit messages from your brain and spinal cord to your voluntary muscles - the ones you can control, like in your arms and legs. At first, this causes mild muscle problems. Some people notice - Trouble walking or running - Trouble writing - Speech problems Eventually, you lose your strength and cannot move. When muscles in your chest fail, you cannot breathe. A breathing machine can help, but most people with ALS die from respiratory failure. The disease usually strikes between age 40 and 60. More men than women get it. No one knows what causes ALS. It can run in families, but usually it strikes at random. There is no cure. Medicines can relieve symptoms and, sometimes, prolong survival. NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
Summary : Anatomy is the science that studies the structure of the body. On this page, you'll find links to descriptions and pictures of the human body's parts and organ systems from head to toe.

Summary : If you have coronary artery disease, the arteries in your heart are narrowed or blocked by a sticky material called plaque. Angioplasty is a procedure to restore blood flow through the artery. You have angioplasty in a hospital. The doctor threads a thin tube through a blood vessel in the arm or groin up to the involved site in the artery. The tube has a tiny balloon on the end. When the tube is in place, the doctor inflates the balloon to push the plaque outward against the wall of the artery. This widens the artery and restores blood flow. Doctors may use angioplasty to - Reduce chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart - Minimize damage to heart muscle from a heart attack Many people go home the day after angioplasty, and are able to return to work within a week of coming home. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Anthrax is a disease caused by Bacillus anthracis, a germ that lives in soil. Many people know about it from the 2001 bioterror attacks. In the attacks, someone purposely spread anthrax through the U.S. mail. This killed five people and made 22 sick. Anthrax is rare. It affects animals such as cattle, sheep, and goats more often than people. People can get anthrax from contact with infected animals, wool, meat, or hides. It can cause three forms of disease in people. They are - Cutaneous, which affects the skin. People with cuts or open sores can get it if they touch the bacteria. - Inhalation, which affects the lungs. You can get this if you breathe in spores of the bacteria. - Gastrointestinal, which affects the digestive system. You can get it by eating infected meat. Antibiotics often cure anthrax if it is diagnosed early. But many people don't know they have anthrax until it is too late to treat. A vaccine to prevent anthrax is available for people in the military and others at high risk. NIH: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases

Fear and anxiety are part of life. You may feel anxious before you take a test or walk down a dark street. This kind of anxiety is useful - it can make you more alert or careful. It usually ends soon after you are out of the situation that caused it. But for millions of people in the United States, the anxiety does not go away, and gets worse over time. They may have chest pains or nightmares. They may even be afraid to leave home. These people have anxiety disorders. Types include - Panic disorder - Obsessive-compulsive disorder - Post-traumatic stress disorder - Phobias - Generalized anxiety disorder Treatment can involve medicines, therapy or both. NIH: National Institute of Mental Health

Please provide more context for this question as "antibacterial drugs" can refer to various types of medications used to treat bacterial infections. It would be helpful if you could specify what type of antibiotic drug(s) and infection(s) you are referring to.
What types of neurological conditions may cause difficulties with speaking?
answers_clean: Acne vulgaris is a common chronic skin condition that affects up to 85% of people during their lifetime . It typically appears in adolescence but can also occur at any age. The exact cause of acne remains unknown; however, it is believed to be related to several factors including genetics, hormones, diet, stress, certain medications, and overgrowth of normal skin bacteria called Propionibacterium acnes .

Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a type of cancer that affects the blood cells in the bone marrow. It occurs when normal precursor cells fail to mature into white blood cells called neutrophils. Instead, these abnormal cells accumulate and crowd out healthy blood cells, leading to an overproduction of immature white blood cells known as blast cells or myeloblasts . AML can occur at any age but is more common in adults aged 65 years or older. The exact cause of AML is unknown, although certain genetic factors may increase the risk of developing this condition. Symptoms typically include fatigue, weight loss, bruising, bleeding, and frequent infections. Treatment options for AML vary depending on the specific subtype of the disease and may involve chemotherapy, radiation therapy, stem cell transplantation, targeted therapies, or supportive care.

The adrenal glands are small organs located above the kidneys that produce hormones such as cortisol, aldosterone, and sex steroids . There are two types of adrenocortical cells in the adrenal cortex: zona glomerulosa cells, which secrete mineralocorticoid hormones; and zona fasciculate reticularis cells, which secrete glucocorticoid hormones .

Yes, I do. Alcohol is a psychoactive substance that can be addictive in nature. It has been linked to various health problems such as liver disease, heart disease, certain types of cancer, mental illnesses, and social issues. The effects of alcohol consumption vary depending on factors such as age, gender, weight, and drinking patterns. Excessive alcohol use can lead to impaired judgment, poor decision-making skills, and increased risky behaviors.

Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is an irreversible neurodegenerative disorder that causes dementia in older adults . It was first described by Dr. Alois Alzheimer in 1906 as “a peculiar form of presenile insanity” . AD affects approximately 5% of people aged over 85 years worldwide , with more than one-third of all cases occurring in high-income countries such as the United States and Europe .

Anal cancer refers to malignant tumors that arise from cells of the anus. It can be caused by various factors such as human papillomavirus infection, smoking, chronic anal fissures or fistulas, inflammatory bowel disease, and immunosuppression. The most common symptom of early-stage anal cancer is rectal bleeding, which may also occur in other conditions such as hemorrhoids. Other symptoms include pain during defecation, itching, lumps around the anus, and discharge. Diagnosis typically involves physical examination, digital rectal exam, biopsy, imaging tests such as CT scans and MRIs, and laboratory testing for viruses like HPV. Treatment options depend on the stage of the cancer but often involve surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, and targeted therapies .

An anesthetic is a drug that induces numbness in a specific part of the body or unconsciousness during surgery or other painful procedures. It can be administered through various routes such as intravenously, by injection into the spinal cord, or via inhalation. There are three main types of anesthetics: general, regional, and local. General anesthetics cause loss of consciousness, while regional anesthetics numb a specific area of the body, often used for limbs or abdomen surgeries. Local anesthetics block pain at the site of the procedure, which is commonly used to numb small areas of skin before minor surgical procedures like stitches .

Animal diseases can have an impact on human health in several ways. Some animal diseases can be transmitted to humans directly through contact with infected animals or their bodily fluids. Other animal diseases may not be able to infect people themselves but could still affect human health by reducing food safety or causing economic losses for farmers and workers in agriculture.

Yes, I do! Antibiotics are drugs that kill or inhibit bacteria. They can be used to treat bacterial infections such as pneumonia, urinary tract infections, and skin abscesses. It is important to use antibiotics responsibly because overuse of them can lead to antibiotic resistance, which makes it harder for doctors to find effective treatments for certain types of infections.

Aphasia refers to a group of communication disorders that occur after damage or injury to the brain's language centers. It can affect speaking, understanding, reading, writing, and other aspects of language use. The severity of symptoms varies depending on the location and extent of the brain damage.
answers_noisy: Spots refer to small areas of discoloration or pigmentation on the skin that may be visible from birth or develop later in life. They can appear anywhere on the body and range in color from black, brown, gray, blue, pink, red, white, yellow, or orange. Most spots are benign (noncancerous) but some can indicate a potential health issue such as melanoma, which is why it's important for individuals with new spots to see a healthcare provider for evaluation.

An allergy is an adverse immune response to a substance or stimulus that is harmless to most people. It occurs when the body's immune system overreacts to a specific substance called an allergen. Allergic reactions can be caused by inhaled allergens such as pollen, dust mites, pet dander, mold spores; ingested allergens like certain foods (e.g., peanuts); contact with skin allergens including latex, poison ivy, metals; or exposure to airborne allergens such as insect venom. The symptoms of an allergic reaction range from mild to severe and may include sneezing, itchy eyes, runny nose, hives, swelling, asthma attacks, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, difficulty breathing, wheeze, cough, chest tightness, low blood pressure, fainting, and even death.

Adrenal disorders can affect or be affected by several other endocrine glands in the body. The hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenocortical axis (HPA) is an important neuroendocrine pathway that regulates various physiological processes such as stress response, metabolism, immunity, reproduction, and behavior . In this system, the hypothalamic peptides corticotropin releasing hormone (CRH) and arginine vasopressin stimulate the pituitary gland to produce adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), which then travels through the bloodstream to reach the outer layer of the adrenal cortex where it activates enzymes involved in steroidogenesis resulting in production of glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids .

Alcohol is a substance that can be consumed in many forms such as beer, wine, spirits, and liquor. It contains ethanol, which is a psychoactive ingredient. Excessive consumption of alcohol has been linked to various health problems including liver disease, heart disease, cancer, mental illnesses, and accidents .

Dementia is a progressive cognitive decline severe enough to impair daily function. It can be caused by various factors including genetics, lifestyle choices such as smoking or alcohol use, infections like HIV, head injuries, certain medications, and underlying health conditions like diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, depression, anxiety, sleep disorders, thyroid disease, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, stroke, transient ischemic attack (TIA), vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, frontotemporal dementia, mixed dementia, and multiple sclerosis.

Cancer of the anal canal refers to malignant tumors arising from the squamous epithelial cells lining the anal canal. It can be classified as either non-metastatic or metastatic disease, with the former being more common. The most common symptom of anal canal cancer is rectal bleeding, which may appear as bright red blood streaks on toilet paper after bowel movements. Other symptoms include pain or discomfort during bowel movements, itching around the anus, swelling or growths around the anus, changes in stool habits, weight loss, and fatigue. Diagnosis typically involves physical examination, digital rectal exam (DRE), endoscopy, biopsy, imaging tests such as CT scans and MRIs, and laboratory testing including blood work and urine analysis. Treatment options for anal canal cancer depend on various factors, including stage at diagnosis, overall health status, age, and personal preferences. Common treatment methods include surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapies, immunotherapies, and clinical trials .

Anesthetics are medications that block pain sensation during surgery or other procedures. They can be administered through inhalation (gas), injection into a vein (intravenous), under the skin (subcutaneous), or directly into the site of the procedure (local). The type of anesthesia used depends on the type of surgery being performed, the patient's overall health status, and their individual needs.

Animals can contract certain diseases that can also be transmitted to humans. These include rabies, plague, tularemia, brucellosis, salmonellosis, campylobacteriosis, leptospirosis, echinococcosis (hydatid disease), avian influenza, SARS, monkeypox, and COVID-19.

Antibiotics, also known as antimicrobials or antimicrobial agents, are medications that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria. They are used to treat bacterial infections and can be classified into different groups depending on their mechanism of action .

Yes, there is a type of brain disorder called primary progressive aphasia (PPA) which causes difficulty in language use without significant impairment in other cognitive domains early in its course . PPA is one of the rare forms of dementia and it affects language function primarily while sparing memory and other cognitive abilities for several years after diagnosis. There are three subtypes of PPA including semantic variant, logopenic variant, and nonfluent variant . The symptoms vary depending on the affected area of the brain but all lead to difficulties with speaking and understanding language.
answers_repaired: As a medical professional, I can tell you that skin lesion is any change in the normal structure of the skin. It could be a growth or a sore anywhere on your body, including your hair, nails, mouth, genitals, and anus. Skin lesions may appear as birthmarks, moles, spots, bumps, rashes, discolorations, scaly patches, sores, ulcers, lumps, nodules, tumors, or warts. They can also take many different forms such as papule (a small bump), macule (an area of color change without elevation), plaque (flat patch with raised edges), vesicle (fluid-filled sac), blisters, pustule (pus-filled sac), nodule (solid swelling), wheals (red-white hives), erythema (redness of the skin), ecchymosis (bruise) and calluses (thickening of the skin due to repeated friction).

Allergic reactions occur when an allergen triggers an immune response in the body that is different from a normal reaction to a harmful substance. This type of immune response can cause symptoms such as sneezing, itching, rashes, swelling, or asthma attacks. Other types of hypersensitivity reactions include contact dermatitis (a skin reaction), anaphylaxis (a severe allergic reaction that affects several systems in the body at once), drug hypersensitivity (an abnormal response to certain medications), and delayed-type hypersensitivity (a cellular immune response that occurs 24–72 hours after exposure).

Adrenal disorders can have significant effects on other endocrine glands due to their complex interactions within the body's endocrine system. The hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenocortical axis (HPA) is one of the most important neuroendocrine axes that regulates various physiological processes such as stress response, metabolism, reproduction, and immunity . In this pathway, the HPA axis stimulates the release of corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), which signals the pituitary gland to produce adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH). ACTH then travels through the bloodstream to reach the outer layer of the adrenal cortex, where it binds to receptors on cells and triggers them to synthesize glucocorticoids (GCs) and mineralocorticoid aldosterone.

Yes, alcohol is a psychoactive substance that can have both positive and negative effects on human health. It has been shown to increase the risk of certain types of cancer, liver disease, heart disease, mental illnesses such as depression and anxiety, and accidents due to impaired cognitive function . Chronic heavy drinking may also lead to brain damage or dementia in some cases .

Dementia is a progressive cognitive impairment that affects memory, language, problem-solving skills, mood or behavior . It can be caused by several factors including genetics, lifestyle choices such as smoking tobacco, alcohol consumption, physical inactivity, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus type 2, depression, low educational level, head injuries, viral infection, stroke, brain tumors, certain medications, and air pollution exposure .

Cancer of the anal canal refers to malignant tumors that arise from the epithelial cells lining the anal canal. It can be classified as either squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) or adenocarcinoma depending on its histological origin. SCC accounts for about 85%-90% of all cases while adenocarcinomas account for only 10%-15%. Other rare types include verrucous carcinoma, basaloid carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, and small cell carcinoma .

Anesthetics are medications that induce loss of consciousness (general anesthesia) or block sensation in a specific part of the body (local or regional anesthesia). They can be administered by various routes such as intravenously, inhalation, or topically. The choice of route depends on factors such as the type of surgery being performed, the patient's health status, and their preference.

Yes, there are several zoonotic (transmissible between animals and people) diseases that can be transmitted to humans from animals. Some examples include rabies, plague, brucellosis, Ebola virus disease, avian influenza A (H5N1), and SARS-CoV-2 (the virus responsible for COVID-19).

Antibiotics are antimicrobials that inhibit or kill bacteria. They work by interfering with essential processes such as cell wall synthesis, protein synthesis, DNA replication, or metabolism (1). The choice of an appropriate antibiotic depends on several factors including the specific pathogen involved, its susceptibility profile, patient characteristics, and potential adverse effects associated with treatment.

There are several neurological disorders that can lead to speech impairment or dysfunction. These include stroke, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), multiple sclerosis, cerebral palsy, traumatic brain injury, dementia, epilepsy, and head trauma.
reference_answers: Acne is a common skin disease that causes pimples. Pimples form when hair follicles under your skin clog up. Most pimples form on the face, neck, back, chest, and shoulders. Anyone can get acne, but it is common in teenagers and young adults. It is not serious, but it can cause scars. No one knows exactly what causes acne. Hormone changes, such as those during the teenage years and pregnancy, probably play a role. There are many myths about what causes acne. Chocolate and greasy foods are often blamed, but there is little evidence that foods have much effect on acne in most people. Another common myth is that dirty skin causes acne; however, blackheads and pimples are not caused by dirt. Stress doesn't cause acne, but stress can make it worse. If you have acne - Clean your skin gently - Try not to touch your skin - Avoid the sun Treatments for acne include medicines and creams. NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases

Leukemia is cancer of the white blood cells. White blood cells help your body fight infection. Your blood cells form in your bone marrow. In leukemia, however, the bone marrow produces abnormal white blood cells. These cells crowd out the healthy blood cells, making it hard for blood to do its work. In acute myeloid leukemia (AML), there are too many of a specific type of white blood cell called a myeloblast. AML is the most common type of acute leukemia in adults. This type of cancer usually gets worse quickly if it is not treated. Possible risk factors include smoking, previous chemotherapy treatment, and exposure to radiation. Symptoms of AML include: - Fever - Shortness of breath - Easy bruising or bleeding - Bleeding under the skin - Weakness or feeling tired - Weight loss or loss of appetite Tests that examine the blood and bone marrow diagnose AML. Treatments include chemotherapy, other drugs, radiation therapy, stem cell transplants, and targeted therapy. Targeted therapy uses substances that attack cancer cells without harming normal cells. Once the leukemia is in remission, you need additional treatment to make sure that it does not come back. NIH: National Cancer Institute

The adrenal glands are small glands located on top of each kidney. They produce hormones that you can't live without, including sex hormones and cortisol. Cortisol helps you respond to stress and has many other important functions. With adrenal gland disorders, your glands make too much or not enough hormones. In Cushing's syndrome, there's too much cortisol, while with Addison's disease, there is too little. Some people are born unable to make enough cortisol. Causes of adrenal gland disorders include - Genetic mutations - Tumors including pheochromocytomas - Infections - A problem in another gland, such as the pituitary, which helps to regulate the adrenal gland - Certain medicines Treatment depends on which problem you have. Surgery or medicines can treat many adrenal gland disorders. NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

Summary : If you are like many Americans, you drink alcohol at least occasionally. For many people, moderate drinking is probably safe. It may even have health benefits, including reducing your risk of certain heart problems. For most women and for most people over 65, moderate drinking is no more than three drinks a day or seven drinks per week. For men under 65, it is no more than four drinks a day or 14 drinks per week. Some people should not drink at all, including alcoholics, children, pregnant women, people taking certain medicines, and people with certain medical conditions. If you have questions about whether it is safe for you to drink, speak with your health care provider. Anything more than moderate drinking can be risky. Heavy drinking can lead to alcoholism and alcohol abuse, as well as injuries, liver disease, heart disease, cancer, and other health problems. It can also cause problems at home, at work, and with friends. NIH: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia among older people. Dementia is a brain disorder that seriously affects a person's ability to carry out daily activities. AD begins slowly. It first involves the parts of the brain that control thought, memory and language. People with AD may have trouble remembering things that happened recently or names of people they know. A related problem, mild cognitive impairment (MCI), causes more memory problems than normal for people of the same age. Many, but not all, people with MCI will develop AD. In AD, over time, symptoms get worse. People may not recognize family members. They may have trouble speaking, reading or writing. They may forget how to brush their teeth or comb their hair. Later on, they may become anxious or aggressive, or wander away from home. Eventually, they need total care. This can cause great stress for family members who must care for them. AD usually begins after age 60. The risk goes up as you get older. Your risk is also higher if a family member has had the disease. No treatment can stop the disease. However, some drugs may help keep symptoms from getting worse for a limited time. NIH: National Institute on Aging

The anus is where stool leaves your body when you go to the bathroom. It is made up of your outer layers of skin and the end of your large intestine. Anal cancer is a disease in which cancer cells form in the tissues of the anus. Anal cancer is rare. It is more common in smokers and people over 50. You are also at higher risk if you have HPV, have anal sex, or have many sexual partners. Symptoms include bleeding, pain, or lumps in the anal area. Anal itching and discharge can also be signs of anal cancer. Doctors use tests that examine the anus to diagnose anal cancer. They include a physical exam, endoscopy, ultrasound, and biopsy. Treatments include radiation therapy, chemotherapy, and surgery. NIH: National Cancer Institute

If you are having surgery, your doctor will give you medicine called an anesthetic. Anesthetics reduce or prevent pain. There are three main types: - Local - numbs one small area of the body. You stay awake and alert. - Regional - blocks pain in an area of the body, such an arm or leg. A common type is epidural anesthesia, which is often used during childbirth. - General - makes you unconscious. You do not feel any pain, and you do not remember the procedure afterwards. You may also get a mild sedative to relax you. You stay awake but may not remember the procedure afterwards. Sedation can be used with or without anesthesia. The type of anesthesia or sedation you get depends on many factors. They include the procedure you are having and your current health.

Animal diseases that people can catch are called zoonoses. Many diseases affecting humans can be traced to animals or animal products. You can get a disease directly from an animal, or indirectly, through the environment. Farm animals can carry diseases. If you touch them or things they have touched, like fencing or buckets, wash your hands thoroughly. Adults should make sure children who visit farms or petting zoos wash up as well. Though they may be cute and cuddly, wild animals may carry germs, viruses, and parasites. Deer and deer mice carry ticks that cause Lyme disease. Some wild animals may carry rabies. Enjoy wildlife from a distance. Pets can also make you sick. Reptiles pose a particular risk. Turtles, snakes and iguanas can transmit Salmonella bacteria to their owners. You can get rabies from an infected dog or toxoplasmosis from handling kitty litter of an infected cat. The chance that your dog or cat will make you sick is small. You can reduce the risk by practicing good hygiene, keeping pet areas clean and keeping your pets' shots up-to-date.

Summary : Antibiotics are powerful medicines that fight bacterial infections. Used properly, antibiotics can save lives. They either kill bacteria or keep them from reproducing. Your body's natural defenses can usually take it from there. Antibiotics do not fight infections caused by viruses, such as - Colds - Flu - Most coughs and bronchitis - Sore throats, unless caused by strep If a virus is making you sick, taking antibiotics may do more harm than good. Using antibiotics when you don't need them, or not using them properly, can add to antibiotic resistance. This happens when bacteria change and become able to resist the effects of an antibiotic. When you take antibiotics, follow the directions carefully. It is important to finish your medicine even if you feel better. If you stop treatment too soon, some bacteria may survive and re-infect you. Do not save antibiotics for later or use someone else's prescription. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

Aphasia is a disorder caused by damage to the parts of the brain that control language. It can make it hard for you to read, write, and say what you mean to say. It is most common in adults who have had a stroke. Brain tumors, infections, injuries, and dementia can also cause it. The type of problem you have and how bad it is depends on which part of your brain is damaged and how much damage there is. There are four main types: - Expressive aphasia - you know what you want to say, but you have trouble saying or writing what you mean - Receptive aphasia - you hear the voice or see the print, but you can't make sense of the words - Anomic aphasia - you have trouble using the correct word for objects, places, or events - Global aphasia - you can't speak, understand speech, read, or write Some people recover from aphasia without treatment. Most, however, need language therapy as soon as possible. NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke

answers_clean: Yes, I do! A1C is a blood test that measures your average blood glucose levels over the past 2 to 3 months. It can help diagnose diabetes or predict how well someone with diabetes is managing their blood sugar control. The normal range for A1C is less than 5.7%. An A1C level of 6.5% or higher suggests diabetes.

An acoustic neuroma, also known as vestibular schwannoma or schwannoma of the eighth cranial nerve, is a benign tumor that arises from Schwann cells in the myelin sheath surrounding the vestibulocochlear nerve . It typically presents with unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and balance problems. Diagnosis can be made through audiometry, MRI, and surgical biopsy if necessary. Treatment options include observation for small tumors, microsurgical resection to remove the tumor, stereotactic radiosurgery, or chemotherapy .

Addison disease, also known as primary adrenocortical insufficiency or hypoadrenalism, is an autoimmune disorder that affects the adrenal glands' cortex, resulting in decreased production of aldosterone and glucocorticoids . It can occur at any age but has a bimodal distribution with peak incidence between 15–30 years old and after 40 years old . The most common cause of Addison disease worldwide is tuberculosis; however, it may be idiopathic in up to half of cases in developed countries .

Yes, I do have information about advance directives. An advance directive is a legal document that outlines your wishes regarding life-sustaining treatment in case you become unable to make decisions for yourself due to illness or injury. There are two types of advance directives: (1) living will and (2) durable power of attorney for health care. A living will expresses your wishes regarding life-sustaining treatments such as mechanical ventilation, artificial feeding, hydration, and pain management. It can also outline other preferences related to end-of-life care. A durable power of attorney for healthcare designates a person who will make medical decisions on your behalf if you are unable to communicate. These documents help ensure that your wishes are carried out during end-of-life care.

Alcohol abuse disorder (AAD), also known as alcohol use disorder (AUD), refers to a pattern of excessive or problematic alcohol consumption that causes harm to one's health, social relationships, or occupational functioning. It is characterized by persistent or recurrent symptoms within a 12-month period, including uncontrolled drinking, preoccupation with alcohol, continued use despite negative consequences, and failure to meet important obligations due to drinking .

Amblyopia is a condition in which one eye has decreased vision acuity that cannot be explained by structural abnormalities of the eye or optical components alone. It is also known as "lazy eye" because it causes reduced vision in only one eye while the other eye may have normal vision.

An anal disorder refers to any condition that affects the normal function of the anus or rectum. These disorders can be congenital, acquired through injury or disease, or idiopathic with no clear cause. Examples of anal disorders include hemorrhoids, fissures, fistulas, abscesses, polyps, incontinence, and fecal incontinence .

An aneurysm refers to a localized dilation or bulging of an artery due to weakening of its wall. It can occur in any blood vessel but most commonly affects medium-sized arteries such as the abdominal aorta, cerebral arteries, popliteal arteries, coronary arteries, and pulmonary arteries . The formation of an aneurysm is often associated with genetic factors, connective tissue disorders, infection, inflammation, trauma, or vascular malformations. If left untreated, it may rupture, leading to life-threatening bleeding .

Ankle injuries and disorders can be defined as any damage or abnormalities of the ankles that affect their structure and function . The most common types of ankle injuries include sprains, fractures, dislocations, tendinitis, bursitis, arthritis, and osteoporosis . These conditions can result from trauma such as falls, sports injuries, repetitive use, or underlying health issues such as diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis . Symptoms may vary depending on the type and severity of the injury but typically involve pain, swelling, stiffness, weakness, instability, or loss of range of motion in the affected area . Treatment options depend on the specific diagnosis but often involve rest, ice, compression, elevation, physical therapy, medications, bracing, or surgery .

Yes, I do have information about antidepressant medications. They belong to a class of drugs called selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). SSRIs work by increasing levels of serotonin in the brain, which can help improve mood symptoms associated with depression. Some common side effects include nausea, headache, dizziness, sleep problems, anxiety, and sexual dysfunction. It is important for patients to discuss any concerns they may have with their healthcare provider before starting an SSRI medication.
answers_noisy: A1C is short for glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c). It is an important blood test that provides information about your average blood glucose levels over the past 2 to 3 months. The normal range of HbA1c is between 4% and 5%. If you have diabetes or other conditions that can affect your blood sugar levels, your doctor may recommend having this test done regularly to monitor how well your treatment plan is working. High HbA1c levels can indicate poor control of your blood sugar, which can lead to serious health problems such as heart disease, kidney damage, blindness, and nerve damage. On the other hand, low HbA1c levels can also be problematic because they can increase the risk of hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), which can cause symptoms such as dizziness, confusion, weakness, seizures, coma, and even death in severe cases.

Acoustic neuroma (AN) is also known as vestibular schwannoma or neurinoma. It is a benign tumor of Schwann cells in the eighth cranial nerve . ANs can be divided into two types: intrameatal and intracanalicular. Intrameatal ANs occur within the internal auditory canal (IAC), while intracanalicular ANs extend from the IAC to the distal portion of the eighth cranial nerve .

Adrenal insufficiency (AI) or Addison's disease is a rare endocrine disorder characterized by chronic adrenocortical deficiencies of mineralocorticoids and glucocorticoids due to damage or destruction of the cortex of both adrenals .

Yes, I do have information about advanced directives. An advanced directive is a legal document that outlines your wishes regarding life-sustaining treatment in case you become unable to make decisions for yourself due to illness or injury. It can include instructions related to resuscitation, mechanical ventilation, feeding tubes, hydration, and other forms of life support.

Alcohol abuse is caused by various factors such as genetic predisposition, environmental influences, mental health disorders, and personal experiences. Treatment for alcohol abuse typically involves a combination of behavioral therapy, medication, and support groups.

Amblyopia, also known as lazy eye, is a neurodevelopmental disorder of the visual system that causes reduced vision in one or both eyes. It occurs when the brain does not properly develop connections between the eye and the part of the brain responsible for processing visual information due to an abnormality in the input from one eye (strabismus) or both eyes (refractive error). The condition can affect children and adults but it most commonly affects young children during the developmental period of life. If left untreated, amblyopia may cause permanent loss of vision.

Aneurysmal disease refers to conditions involving abnormal dilatations of blood vessels, known as aneurysms. These can occur in any type of artery but are most commonly found in the cerebral or abdominal circulation. The formation of an aneurysm typically involves weakening of the vessel wall due to genetic predisposition, inflammation, infection, trauma, or other factors. As the weakened area expands over time, it may eventually rupture, leading to life-threatening bleeding.

An aneurysm is a bulge or balloon-like enlargement in a blood vessel that can occur anywhere in the body but most commonly occurs in the brain (brain aneurysms) or in other arteries such as those supplying the legs (aortic aneurysms). Aneurysms may be caused by various factors including genetic conditions, high blood pressure, infection, or trauma. They can lead to serious health problems if they rupture, causing life-threatening bleeding. It is important for individuals with risk factors for aneurysms to work closely with their healthcare providers to monitor their condition and take steps to prevent them from developing.

An ankle injury can be caused by various factors such as trauma or sports injuries. The most common symptoms of ankle injuries include pain, swelling, bruising, difficulty walking, stiffness, and weakness in the affected limb. Depending on the severity of the injury, it may require rest, ice, compression, elevation (RICE) treatment, physical therapy, medications, or even surgery to alleviate these symptoms.

Yes, I can provide information about antihypertensive medications. Antihypertensives are drugs used to treat high blood pressure (BP). They work by relaxing or dilating the blood vessels, which reduces BP. There are several classes of antihypertensive medications available, including angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEIs), beta blockers, calcium channel blockers (CCBs), diuretics, alpha blockers, vasodilators, and renin-angiotensin aldosterone system (RAAS) blockers .
answers_repaired: Yes, I do! A1C is also known as glycated hemoglobin or HbA1c. It is an important blood test that provides information about your average blood glucose levels over the past 2 to 3 months. The normal range for most people is between 4% (6.0%) and 5.9% (7.8%). If you have diabetes mellitus, it can be used to monitor how well you are controlling your blood sugar with treatment.

Acoustic neuromas (ANs) are benign tumors that arise from Schwann cells in the vestibulocochlear nerve . They can be classified as either intrameatal or intracanalicular depending on their location within the internal auditory canal (IAC). Intrameatal ANs originate from the lower part of the IAC near the cochlea, while intracanalicular ANs develop from the upper portion of the IAC near the superior vestibular nerve.

Addison’s disease (AD) is an autoimmune disorder that affects the adrenal cortex, leading to insufficiency of glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoid hormones . It can occur at any age but usually presents in early childhood or middle-aged adults with symptoms such as fatigue, weight loss, skin hyperpigmentation, low blood pressure, hypoglycemia, and salt cravings . Diagnosis involves measuring plasma renin activity, serum aldosterone levels, urinary steroid metabolites, and ACTH levels . Treatment typically includes lifelong replacement therapy with glucocorticoids and sometimes mineralocorticoids .

As a healthcare professional, it is important to ensure that patients have access to accurate and reliable health information. This can include providing them with educational materials or referring them to other resources where they can find the information they need. It is also important to communicate clearly and effectively with patients so that they understand their treatment options and can make informed decisions about their care.

Alcohol abuse is defined as an excessive or problematic pattern of alcohol consumption that leads to negative consequences in one's life . The exact cause of alcohol abuse is not fully understood but may be influenced by genetic, environmental, psychological, social, and cultural factors. Genetic factors play a role in determining individual differences in sensitivity to alcohol's effects and predisposition towards addiction. Environmental factors such as childhood trauma, family history of substance use disorder, peer pressure, and availability of alcohol can also contribute to the development of alcohol abuse. Psychological factors such as anxiety, depression, personality traits (e.g., impulsivity), and co-occurring mental health disorders can increase risk for alcohol abuse. Social and cultural factors including gender norms, stigma associated with drinking, and cultural expectations around alcohol consumption can influence patterns of alcohol use.

Amblyopia refers to decreased vision acuity due to abnormal visual experience during early childhood that results in permanent loss of vision . It typically affects one eye but may affect both eyes. The condition occurs when the brain favors one eye over another for input into the visual pathway, resulting in reduced development of the weaker eye's connections with the brain.

An aneurysmal disorder refers to any condition that involves abnormal dilatation of an artery or vein. This can include true aneurysms, pseudoaneurysms, ectasia (abnormal enlargement), dissection (tearing within the vessel wall) and malformations such as arteriovenous fistulas and venous varicose conditions. These disorders can occur in various locations throughout the body including the brain, thoracic, abdominal and peripheral vasculature. They may be congenital or acquired through disease processes, trauma, infection or iatrogenic causes.

An aneurysm refers to a localized dilation or bulging of the arterial wall that occurs due to weakening of the vessel's wall. This condition can occur in any artery but most commonly affects the abdominal aorta. Aneurysms may be asymptomatic or present with symptoms such as pain, back spasms, nausea, vomiting, shock, or rupture which can lead to life-threatening bleeding.

Ankle injuries refer to damage or harm done to the structures of the ankle joint such as bones, ligaments, tendons, muscles, nerves, blood vessels, cartilage, and skin. These can be caused by trauma, overuse, or other factors that affect the normal function of the ankle joint. Disorders, on the other hand, are abnormalities in the structure or function of the ankle joint that may not necessarily involve injury but could still cause pain and dysfunction. Some examples of ankle disorders include arthritis, tarsal tunnel syndrome, and Achilles tendonitis. It is important for individuals who experience ankle pain or difficulty performing daily activities due to ankle problems to consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment.

Yes, I do! There are several classes of drugs that can be used to treat hypertension (high blood pressure), including angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEIs), beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, diuretics, alpha blockers, vasodilators, renin inhibitors, and adrenergic neuron blocking agents. The choice of medication depends on factors such as age, other health conditions, and whether or not the patient has kidney disease. It is important to work with your healthcare provider to determine which type of medication will be most effective for you.
reference_answers: Summary : A1C is a blood test for type 2 diabetes and prediabetes. It measures your average blood glucose, or blood sugar, level over the past 3 months. Doctors may use the A1C alone or in combination with other diabetes tests to make a diagnosis. They also use the A1C to see how well you are managing your diabetes. This test is different from the blood sugar checks that people with diabetes do every day. Your A1C test result is given in percentages. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been: - A normal A1C level is below 5.7 percent - Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4 percent. Having prediabetes is a risk factor for getting type 2 diabetes. People with prediabetes may need retests every year. - Type 2 diabetes is above 6.5 percent - If you have diabetes, you should have the A1C test at least twice a year. The A1C goal for many people with diabetes is below 7. It may be different for you. Ask what your goal should be. If your A1C result is too high, you may need to change your diabetes care plan. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

An acoustic neuroma is a benign tumor that develops on the nerve that connects the ear to the brain. The tumor usually grows slowly. As it grows, it presses against the hearing and balance nerves. At first, you may have no symptoms or mild symptoms. They can include - Loss of hearing on one side - Ringing in ears - Dizziness and balance problems The tumor can also eventually cause numbness or paralysis of the face. If it grows large enough, it can press against the brain, becoming life-threatening. Acoustic neuroma can be difficult to diagnose, because the symptoms are similar to those of middle ear problems. Ear exams, hearing tests, and scans can show if you have it. If the tumor stays small, you may only need to have it checked regularly. If you do need treatment, surgery and radiation are options. If the tumors affect both hearing nerves, it is often because of a genetic disorder called neurofibromatosis. NIH: National Institute on Deafness and Communication Disorders

Your adrenal glands are just above your kidneys. The outside layer of these glands makes hormones that help your body respond to stress and regulate your blood pressure and water and salt balance. Addison disease happens if the adrenal glands don't make enough of these hormones. A problem with your immune system usually causes Addison disease. The immune system mistakenly attacks your own tissues, damaging your adrenal glands. Other causes include infections and cancer. Symptoms include - Weight loss - Muscle weakness - Fatigue that gets worse over time - Low blood pressure - Patchy or dark skin Lab tests can confirm that you have Addison disease. If you don't treat it, it can be fatal. You will need to take hormone pills for the rest of your life. If you have Addison disease, you should carry an emergency ID. It should say that you have the disease, list your medicines and say how much you need in an emergency. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

Summary : What kind of medical care would you want if you were too ill or hurt to express your wishes? Advance directives are legal documents that allow you to spell out your decisions about end-of-life care ahead of time. They give you a way to tell your wishes to family, friends, and health care professionals and to avoid confusion later on. A living will tells which treatments you want if you are dying or permanently unconscious. You can accept or refuse medical care. You might want to include instructions on - The use of dialysis and breathing machines - If you want to be resuscitated if your breathing or heartbeat stops - Tube feeding - Organ or tissue donation A durable power of attorney for health care is a document that names your health care proxy. Your proxy is someone you trust to make health decisions for you if you are unable to do so. NIH: National Cancer Institute

For most adults, moderate alcohol use is probably not harmful. However, about 18 million adult Americans have an alcohol use disorder. This means that their drinking causes distress and harm. It includes alcoholism and alcohol abuse. Alcoholism, or alcohol dependence, is a disease that causes - Craving - a strong need to drink - Loss of control - not being able to stop drinking once you've started - Physical dependence - withdrawal symptoms - Tolerance - the need to drink more alcohol to feel the same effect With alcohol abuse, you are not physically dependent, but you still have a serious problem. The drinking may cause problems at home, work, or school. It may cause you to put yourself in dangerous situations, or lead to legal or social problems. Another common problem is binge drinking. It is drinking about five or more drinks in two hours for men. For women, it is about four or more drinks in two hours. Too much alcohol is dangerous. Heavy drinking can increase the risk of certain cancers. It can cause damage to the liver, brain, and other organs. Drinking during pregnancy can harm your baby. Alcohol also increases the risk of death from car crashes, injuries, homicide, and suicide. If you want to stop drinking, there is help. Start by talking to your health care provider. Treatment may include medicines, counseling, and support groups. NIH: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism

Amblyopia, or "lazy eye," is the most common cause of visual impairment in children. It happens when an eye fails to work properly with the brain. The eye may look normal, but the brain favors the other eye. In some cases, it can affect both eyes. Causes include - Strabismus - a disorder in which the two eyes don't line up in the same direction - Refractive error in an eye - when one eye cannot focus as well as the other, because of a problem with its shape. This includes nearsightedness, farsightedness, and astigmatism. - Cataract - a clouding in the lens of the eye It can be hard to diagnose amblyopia. It is often found during a routine vision exam. Treatment for amblyopia forces the child to use the eye with weaker vision. There are two common ways to do this. One is to have the child wear a patch over the good eye for several hours each day, over a number of weeks to months. The other is with eye drops that temporarily blur vision. Each day, the child gets a drop of a drug called atropine in the stronger eye. It is also sometimes necessary to treat the underlying cause. This could include glasses or surgery. NIH: National Eye Institute

The anus is the opening of the rectum through which stool passes out of your body. Problems with the anus are common. They include hemorrhoids, abscesses, fissures (cracks), and cancer. You may be embarrassed to talk about your anal troubles. But it is important to let your doctor know, especially if you have pain or bleeding. The more details you can give about your problem, the better your doctor will be able to help you. Treatments vary depending on the particular problem. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

An aneurysm is a bulge or "ballooning" in the wall of an artery. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood from the heart to other parts of the body. If an aneurysm grows large, it can burst and cause dangerous bleeding or even death. Most aneurysms occur in the aorta, the main artery that runs from the heart through the chest and abdomen. Aneurysms also can happen in arteries in the brain, heart and other parts of the body. If an aneurysm in the brain bursts, it causes a stroke. Aneurysms can develop and become large before causing any symptoms. Often doctors can stop aneurysms from bursting if they find and treat them early. They use imaging tests to find aneurysms. Often aneurysms are found by chance during tests done for other reasons. Medicines and surgery are the two main treatments for aneurysms. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Your ankle bone and the ends of your two lower leg bones make up the ankle joint. Your ligaments, which connect bones to one another, stabilize and support it. Your muscles and tendons move it. The most common ankle problems are sprains and fractures. A sprain is an injury to the ligaments. It may take a few weeks to many months to heal completely. A fracture is a break in a bone. You can also injure other parts of the ankle such as tendons, which join muscles to bone, and cartilage, which cushions your joints. Ankle sprains and fractures are common sports injuries.

Summary : Antidepressants are medicines that treat depression. Your doctor can prescribe them for you. They work to balance some of the natural chemicals in our brains. It may take several weeks for them to help. There are several types of antidepressants. You and your doctor may have to try a few before finding what works best for you. Antidepressants may cause mild side effects that usually do not last long. These may include headache, nausea, sleep problems, restlessness, and sexual problems. Tell your doctor if you have any side effects. You should also let your doctor know if you take any other medicines, vitamins, or herbal supplements. It is important to keep taking your medicines, even if you feel better. Do not stop taking your medicines without talking to your doctor. You often need to stop antidepressants gradually. NIH: National Institute of Mental Health
